name: Modena
short_description: BLM rockhounding site in southwestern Utah's Basin and Range province. Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens on open desert terrain near the Nevada border.
description:
Modena sits on Utah BLM land in Iron County, part of the high desert plateau that straddles the Utah-Nevada border. This remote rockhounding area is known for agate and jasper specimens weathered from Tertiary volcanic and sedimentary formations that blanket the region. The terrain is typical of the Great Basin - sparse vegetation, scattered rock outcrops, and accessible washes where agates concentrate after seasonal erosion.

The geological setting here reflects ancient silica-rich volcanism and groundwater alteration during the Miocene and Pliocene epochs. Chalcedony deposits and banded agates formed in cavities and fractures of host rocks, then weathered free and tumbled down to accessible surface exposures. Jasper specimens tend to be less vibrant than southwestern Arizona varieties but are still collectible and valuable for lapidary work.

This location offers genuine backcountry rockhounding without fees or permits - just respect the 25 lb daily limit on BLM land. The site attracts dedicated rockhounders willing to drive remote dirt roads and hike moderate distances to reach productive areas. Weather can be extreme here, and services are sparse, so preparation is essential.

Expect to spend 4-8 hours searching washes, bajadas, and eroded hillsides where specimens naturally concentrate. Best results come from patience and systematic coverage of promising ground. The remoteness and lack of crowds make this an excellent destination for serious collectors seeking solitude and authentic field experience.
history: This region lies within the Basin and Range province where Miocene to Pliocene volcanic and hydrothermal activity deposited silica-rich fluids that formed agate, chalcedony, and jasper nodules in cooling lava flows and sedimentary host rocks. The Tertiary volcanic sequences that underlie Modena have yielded collectible specimens for decades, and the open BLM land here remains largely underexplored compared to better-known rockhounding areas in Arizona and southern Nevada.
tips: Search along dry washes and bajada fans where water erosion naturally concentrates agates and jasper. Focus on eroded hillsides with exposed volcanic outcrops - specimens often weather free and collect in rinse-outs. Bring a magnet to test material - true agate and chalcedony will not respond, but associated iron oxides in specimens may. Early morning and late afternoon light reveals color and luster better on the pale desert surface.
terrain_notes: High desert plateau at approximately 5,200-5,600 ft elevation with sparse creosote and shadscale vegetation. Terrain is gently rolling to broken, with exposed volcanic rock outcrops and loose talus slopes. Walking surfaces vary from compacted dirt to rocky washes to loose gravel. Footing is generally stable but requires sturdy boots for protection from sharp volcanic material. Distances from parking areas to productive ground vary from 0.5 to 3 miles depending on which drainage or wash system you target.
best_season: October - April. Summer heat at this elevation can exceed 95F on exposed desert ground. Winter nights drop below freezing but daytime temperatures are mild and ideal for fieldwork. Spring (March-April) offers good weather, though dust storms are possible. Late fall (October-November) provides excellent conditions with cool mornings and clear skies. Avoid June through August due to heat and occasional monsoon flash floods in washes.
written_directions: From Cedar City (approximately 35 miles east), take US 56 west toward the Nevada border for about 25 miles. Continue on local BLM access roads toward Modena - terrain data will guide final approach via dirt roads. Modena itself is a small unincorporated community with minimal services. Access the best rockhounding areas by driving onto open BLM land and parking at pullouts near washes or hillside exposures. Consult a detailed Utah BLM map or GPS unit for specific road names and coordinates; roads in this area are marked but not always signed. High-clearance or 4WD vehicle strongly recommended for reliability on rough, washboard sections.
road_conditions: Rough washboard dirt roads, some sandy sections, occasional water bars. High-clearance or 4WD recommended. Normal passenger cars may struggle; scout roads first if inexperienced.
parking_notes: No developed parking area. Park at pullouts along BLM roads near productive washes or hillside exposures. Space is informal and minimal - typically room for 1-3 vehicles at any given spot. No facilities or signage.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or powered tools. Hand tools only - crack hammers, chisels, and small pry bars acceptable. Do not damage living plants or disturb archaeological sites. Pack out all trash.

nearest_services: Cedar City, Utah (approximately 35 miles east) - full services including gas, food, water, and lodging. Modena itself has no services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate difficulty terrain with uneven ground, loose volcanic rock, and potential unstable slopes. No wheelchair access. Requires sturdy hiking boots and reasonable fitness for 1-3 mile walks across broken terrain. Rocky surfaces and sharp volcanic material present cutting hazards. Some areas involve scrambling over talus or loose scree. Not suitable for limited mobility or young children without close supervision.

faq.question: What types of agates can I find rockhounding near Modena, Utah?
faq.answer: Modena BLM land produces primarily banded agate and chalcedony specimens, often with subtle earth tones and translucent quality. Jasper finds are also common but typically less colorful than Arizona varieties. Specimens range from grape-sized to fist-sized, with occasional larger pieces. The banding and color variation make them suitable for lapidary cabochons and tumbling.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Modena on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Modena. The area is open BLM land where personal use collection is allowed under FLPMA rules. However, you must follow the 25 lb daily limit, use hand tools only, and never engage in commercial collection or motorized excavation. Always pack out trash and respect the land.

faq.question: How far is Modena rockhounding from Cedar City and what are road conditions like?
faq.answer: Modena is approximately 35 miles west of Cedar City via US 56 and local BLM roads. The final approach involves rough, washboard dirt roads requiring high-clearance or 4WD vehicles. Road conditions vary seasonally - summer dust and spring runoff can affect access. Scout roads first if driving a passenger car, as some sections may be impassable after rain.

faq.question: What is the best time of year for rockhounding at Modena?
faq.answer: October through April offers ideal rockhounding conditions at Modena. Winter nights are cold (freezing is common) but daytime temperatures are mild. Avoid June through August due to intense heat (95F+) and monsoon flash flood risk in washes. Spring (March-April) brings occasional dust storms, while fall provides the most stable weather and clear skies.

faq.question: What tools and gear do I need for rockhounding at remote Modena?
faq.answer: Bring a quality crack hammer, cold chisel, and small pry bar for specimen extraction. Wear sturdy boots (sharp volcanic rock cuts easily), sun protection, and a hat. Carry at least 2-3 liters of water per person - there are no water sources on BLM land. Bring a magnet to test material, GPS unit for navigation, first aid kit, and high-clearance vehicle recommended. Pack all trash out.

faq.question: Is Modena good for beginner rockhounders or do I need experience?
faq.answer: Modena is suitable for beginners with moderate hiking ability and proper preparation. Specimens are accessible on the surface without advanced skills. However, the remote location, lack of cell service, and rough terrain require careful planning - tell someone your location, bring extra water, and ensure vehicle reliability. The moderate difficulty terrain and risk of getting lost make solo exploration inadvisable for inexperienced rockhounders.

meta_title: Modena Rockhounding - Utah BLM Agate & Jasper
